# External Literature Review of the "TEP / Entrodynamics" Framework

## TL;DR

- **Most TEP claims are either standard restatements of established results (Kerr–Newman horizon, Bures/Fisher radial reduction, c/√2 critical speed, SU(1,1) on the Poincaré disk, MSS chaos bound, Riccati derivations of Bose–Einstein / Fermi–Dirac, three-term port budgets) or numerically–coincident identifications across unrelated subfields rather than novel physics; the framework's predictive value over existing literature is therefore low.**
- **Two specific items are plausibly novel as formulated — a quantum speed limit on Tr(ρ³) using F = Tr(ρ³) − γ² as a Fisher-type quantity, and the explicit "moment hierarchy" |dp_k/dt| ≤ 2k√(p_{2k−1} − p_k²) ΔH — but they are direct corollaries of the standard Mandelstam–Tamm + Heisenberg-equation argument applied to the observable ρ^{k−1}, and the rank-2 wedge-product saturating structure is the well-known Anandan–Aharonov two-level saturation in different notation.**
- **Several TEP claims overstep established physics: the Hodge-decomposition / DtN connection to a three-term port budget is not a recognized theorem in passive scattering; the convex-additive ("NSR") reproduction of Haldane statistics from BE + FD does not in general satisfy the Wu equation w^g(1+w)^{1−g} = e^{β(ε−μ)}; and the "self-dual point" coincidences are not a published unifying principle. "Terminal Equivalence Principle" and "Entrodynamics" do not appear in the indexed peer-reviewed literature.**

## Key Findings

| Area | Established literature contains | TEP claim is | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Purity / moment speed limits | Mandelstam–Tamm 1945; Margolus–Levitin 1998; Giovannetti–Lloyd–Maccone, "Quantum limits to dynamical evolution," *Phys. Rev. A* 67, 052109 (2003); [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/quant-ph/0303085) Uzdin–Lutz–Kosloff arXiv:1408.1227 (2014); Bolonek-Lasoń et al. arXiv:1902.02110 (2019); Pires–Cianciaruso–Adesso 2016 (generalized geometric QSL); Hamazaki *PRX Quantum* 3, 020319 (2022) and *Commun. Phys.* 7, 361 (2024) | A natural corollary of MT applied to ρ^{k−1}; specific Tr(ρ³) form not previously published in this exact form; rank-2 wedge saturation = Anandan–Aharonov *PRL* 65, 1697 (1990) | Partial novelty in form, none in mechanism |
| 2. Bures / Fisher / Petz | Bures 1969; Braunstein–Caves *PRL* 72, 3439 (1994); Petz, *Lin. Alg. Appl.* 244, 81 (1996); Chentsov 1982; Dittmann arXiv:quant-ph/9810012 (1999); Petz–Sudár, *J. Math. Phys.* 37, 2662 (1996) | Standard; "budget identity" λ_+ + λ_− = 1 is just the trace-1 normalization | None — restatement |
| 3. Passive scattering / Hodge / DtN | Standard S-matrix unitarity deficits; port-Hamiltonian theory (van der Schaft); Sylvester–Uhlmann 1987; Guo–Fan, *Phys. Rev. B* 110, 205431 (2024); Belishev–Sharafutdinov; Sharafutdinov–Shonkwiler, *J. Geom. Anal.* (2013) | Three-term partition is standard; Hodge-decomposition mapping is **not** an established equivalence | TEP's specific Hodge↔port mapping appears unsupported |
| 4. c/√2, self-dual point | Chicone–Mashhoon, *Class. Quantum Grav.* 21, L139 (2004) and follow-ups 2004–2006 | c/√2 is real but a specific GR result in the *Fermi-coordinate generalized Jacobi equation*, not a universal "self-dual" coincidence; "critical damping" ζ = 1/√2 is in fact the Butterworth / maximally-flat value, not classical critical damping (ζ = 1) | Coincidence-mongering — no unifying principle in literature |
| 5. SU(1,1) / Poincaré disk | Perelomov, "Coherent states for arbitrary Lie group," *Commun. Math. Phys.* 26, 222 (1972); [Project Euclid](https://projecteuclid.org/journals/communications-in-mathematical-physics/volume-26/issue-3/Coherent-states-for-arbitrary-Lie-group/cmp/1103858078.full) Caves–Schumaker, *Phys. Rev. A* 31, 3068 (1985) and Schumaker–Caves, *Phys. Rev. A* 31, 3093 (1985); Chi–Fraas–Tan arXiv:2404.07905 (2024) | Standard for Gaussian / squeezed states; bi-metric comparison with Bures on the qubit Bloch ball is unusual but not wrong | Geometry standard |
| 6. MSS bound, OTOC, capacity | Maldacena–Shenker–Stanford, *JHEP* 08, 106 (2016); SYK saturation; butterfly cone (Mezei–Stanford arXiv:1908.03574) | The factor 1/ln 2 is just a bits-vs-nats conversion; no established theorem links channel-capacity decay rate to λ_L | TEP's identity is dimensional / unit-conversion, not a new result |
| 7. Riccati / Haldane | Standard grand-canonical derivations; Haldane *PRL* 67, 937 (1991); Wu *PRL* 73, 922 (1994); Kaniadakis–Quarati, *Phys. Rev. E* 49, 5103 (1994); Rossani arXiv:hep-th/9507119 (1995); Aringazin–Mazhitov arXiv:cond-mat/0202274 (2002) | Riccati derivation of BE/FD/MB is known (logistic-equation viewpoint, kinetic approach). "Convex NSR" reproduction of Haldane statistics is *not* the Wu equation in general | Likely incorrect for general g |
| 8. Naming / prior programs | "Scale Relativity" = Nottale (1984–present), distinct concept | "Terminal Equivalence Principle" / "Entrodynamics" / "Generalized Scale Relativity" not in indexed literature | No prior published program under these names |
| 9. Kerr–Newman identity | Standard r_± = M ± √(M² − a² − Q²) | Trivial algebraic rearrangement of the textbook horizon formula | No novelty |

## Details

### 1. Quantum speed limits and purity dynamics

**Established literature.** The Mandelstam–Tamm (1945) bound τ_⊥ ≥ ℏπ/(2 ΔE) and the Margolus–Levitin (1998) bound τ_⊥ ≥ ℏπ/(2⟨E − E₀⟩) are the canonical pure-state QSLs. For mixed states there is a large family of bounds: Uhlmann (1992); Giovannetti–Lloyd–Maccone, "Quantum limits to dynamical evolution," *Phys. Rev. A* 67, 052109 (2003); del Campo et al. (2013); Deffner–Lutz (2013); Mondal–Pati (2016); Pires–Cianciaruso–Céleri–Adesso–Soares-Pinto, "Generalized Geometric Quantum Speed Limits," *Phys. Rev. X* 6, 021031 (2016), [ResearchGate](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/330241276_Geometric_quantum_speed_limits_and_short-time_accessibility_to_unitary_operations) which shows every Petz monotone metric generates a valid geometric QSL. For *purity dynamics* specifically: Uzdin–Lutz–Kosloff (arXiv:1408.1227, 2014) derived purity-evolution speed limits [arXiv](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/1408.1227) in Liouville space for Markovian open dynamics; [arxiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1408.1227) Bolonek-Lasoń–Gonera–Kosiński (arXiv:1902.02110, 2019) recast the Mandelstam–Tamm bound in Hilbert–Schmidt operator space to obtain a relative-purity bound; [arxiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1902.02110) Pires–de Oliveira (*Phys. Rev. A* 104, 052223, 2021) gave relative-purity / equilibration-time bounds. Hamazaki's contributions are: (i) "Speed Limits for Macroscopic Transitions" (*PRX Quantum* 3, 020319, 2022; arXiv:2110.09716) — bounds the speed of an observable ⟨O⟩ on a graph via a continuity-equation argument, [arxiv +2](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2110.09716) **not** moments Tr(ρ^k); (ii) "Quantum Velocity Limits for Multiple Observables" (*Phys. Rev. Research* 6, 013018, 2024; arXiv:2305.03190) — simultaneous bounds on several ⟨O_i⟩; [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2305.03190) (iii) "Speed limits to fluctuation dynamics" (*Commun. Phys.* 7, 361, 2024; arXiv:2309.07301), [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.07301) which states explicitly that "inequalities similar to (1) are obtained for even higher-order cumulants" [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/html/2309.07301) — but the cumulants are of the *measurement distribution of an observable A*, not of Tr(ρ^k).

**Assessment of TEP's claim.** The inequality |dγ/dt| ≤ 4√F · ΔH with F = Tr(ρ³) − γ² and γ = Tr(ρ²) is a direct application of the Heisenberg equation dγ/dt = −i Tr([H, ρ²]ρ) = 2i Tr([ρ, H]ρ²) plus Cauchy–Schwarz / Robertson, treating ρ̂ (or ρ²) as an observable. Once written this way, F = Tr(ρ³) − (Tr ρ²)² is precisely the Robertson variance Var_ρ(ρ̂) of the density operator regarded as an observable, and the bound is a Mandelstam–Tamm corollary for the operator ρ in the doubled (Liouville-space) representation — the same technique used by Bolonek-Lasoń et al. (arXiv:1902.02110) and Uzdin–Lutz–Kosloff (arXiv:1408.1227). My targeted search found **no prior paper that writes the bound for purity in exactly this Tr(ρ³)-form** or that names F = Tr(ρ³) − γ² as a Fisher-type quantity. The moment hierarchy |dp_k/dt| ≤ 2k √(p_{2k−1} − p_k²) ΔH is its natural extension to Tr(ρ^k) and likewise does not appear in this exact form in the searched literature; it sits adjacent to but distinct from Hamazaki's 2024 cumulant bounds.

The claim that *rank-2 wedge-product* Hamiltonians W = u ∧ v saturate these bounds is the **Anandan–Aharonov, "Geometry of quantum evolution," *Phys. Rev. Lett.* 65, 1697 (1990)** two-level saturation of MT in different notation: in the projective-Hilbert-space geometry, MT is saturated iff the dynamics is confined to span{|ψ⟩, H|ψ⟩}, a 2-plane, equivalently a Bloch great circle. Anandan and Aharonov state explicitly that "the integral of the uncertainty of energy with respect to time is independent of the particular Hamiltonian used to transport the quantum system along a given curve in the projective Hilbert space." [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_speed_limit) [Tel Aviv University](https://cris.tau.ac.il/en/publications/geometry-of-quantum-evolution/) Hörnedal–Carabba–Takahashi–del Campo (*Quantum* 7, 1055, 2023; arXiv:2301.04372) explicitly state for operator QSLs that "the OQSL is saturated by any traceless observable involving solely the coupling of the ground state and an excited state" [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/html/2301.04372) — a rank-2 structure equivalent to TEP's wedge u ∧ v.

**Verdict:** mechanism not novel; specific form for Tr(ρ³) and identification of F as a Fisher-type quantity is a plausible new corollary of well-known machinery. Consistent with established physics.

### 2. Bures / Fisher / Petz information geometry

**Established literature.** Bures (1969) introduced the metric; Braunstein–Caves, *Phys. Rev. Lett.* 72, 3439 (1994), showed it equals (¼ times) the quantum Fisher information for the symmetric logarithmic derivative; Petz, *Lin. Alg. Appl.* 244, 81 (1996), classified all monotone Riemannian metrics on density matrices in terms of operator-monotone functions [Semantic Scholar](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Monotone-metrics-on-matrix-spaces-Petz/f5d49df3e6ee19ed1d6be47515a0cad078924dd0) (Morozova–Chentsov functions); Chentsov (1982) proved classical Fisher information is the unique (up to a scalar) monotone metric on probability simplices. [American Mathematical Society](https://www.ams.org/journals/proc/2004-132-03/S0002-9939-03-07175-2/S0002-9939-03-07175-2.pdf) Dittmann (arXiv:quant-ph/9810012, 1999) derived the scalar curvature of the Bures metric; restriction of the Bures metric to the manifold of trace-1 diagonal (commuting) matrices yields exactly the classical Fisher–Rao metric up to the factor ¼ [arxiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/quant-ph/9810012) — this is **explicitly stated** in Dittmann (1999) and in Petz–Sudár, *J. Math. Phys.* 37, 2662 (1996). Slater (arXiv:quant-ph/0504066) and Bengtsson (arXiv:quant-ph/0509017) note that the radial (purity) component is the *same* for **every** Petz monotone metric: g_rr = 1/(1−r²), where r is Bloch radius — only the tangential component varies.

**Assessment of TEP's claim.** The radial reduction to Fisher–Rao is a **textbook fact** (Dittmann 1999; Petz–Sudár 1996; Bengtsson 2005). The "budget identity" X² + V² = 1 in this context is just the unit-trace normalization λ_+ + λ_− = 1 for a qubit eigenvalue pair, equivalently the statement that the Bloch radius r ≤ 1. The "G-factor" 1/√(1 − X²) is the radial metric coefficient g_rr = 1/(1 − r²) up to factors of 2. The "self-dual point" r² = ½ corresponds to purity Tr(ρ²) = ½(1 + r²) = ¾ for a qubit — no published geometric significance beyond being the fixed point of f(y) = √(1 − y²).

**Verdict:** entirely standard restatement; no priority. Consistent.

### 3. Passive scattering, port-Hamiltonian, and DtN

**Established literature.** For a passive linear time-invariant network, ‖S‖ ≤ 1 yields at each port the unitarity-deficit identity T_j + R_j + A_j = 1 (transmission + reflection + absorption fractions) — this decomposition is standard in mesoscopic-physics and microwave-engineering textbooks (Beenakker; Pozar; van der Schaft). Guo–Fan (*Phys. Rev. B* 110, 205431, 2024; arXiv:2410.04565) recently gave a complete polyhedral characterization of permissible (T, R, A) eigenvalue triples via Horn's inequalities. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/html/2410.04565) [APS Journals](https://journals.aps.org/prb/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevB.110.205431) Port-Hamiltonian systems theory (van der Schaft; Maschke) is the standard framework for energy-conserving / dissipative interconnection. The Dirichlet-to-Neumann (DtN) operator and Calderón's inverse problem (Calderón 1980; Sylvester–Uhlmann 1987; Uhlmann reviews) recover conductivity from boundary measurements. There is a developed literature on the DtN map for *differential forms* and its relation to Hodge theory: Belishev–Sharafutdinov (*Bull. Sci. Math.* 133, 2008) and Sharafutdinov–Shonkwiler (*J. Geom. Anal.*, 2013) give the "complete Dirichlet-to-Neumann map for differential forms" and Poincaré-duality angles via Hodge decomposition.

**Assessment of TEP's claim.** The three-term port budget x² + w + u = 1 (transmission / reflection / absorption) is the **textbook unitarity-deficit decomposition**, not novel. The claim that this decomposition *maps* onto the Hodge decomposition (co-exact / harmonic / exact) and is "derived from" the DtN operator is **not** a recognized result in the searched literature. The Belishev–Sharafutdinov framework gives a DtN map *between* harmonic forms with prescribed boundary components, but this is a structural decomposition of the operator's domain, not a port-by-port equation. The TEP mapping appears to be a heuristic identification, not a theorem.

**Verdict:** three-term budget standard; Hodge ↔ port mapping not established. Partial inconsistency in the sense that the identification is claimed without published support.

### 4. The c/√2 critical speed and "self-dual points"

**Established literature.** Chicone & Mashhoon (*Class. Quantum Grav.* 21, L139, 2004; arXiv:gr-qc/0406118) introduce V_c = c/√2 in the **generalized Jacobi equation** in Fermi coordinates: the first-order tidal force for one-dimensional motion is multiplied by (1 − 2V²/c²), giving sign reversal at V = c/√2. [arxiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/gr-qc/0406118) The same critical speed reappears in Chicone–Mashhoon, "Tidal acceleration of ultrarelativistic particles," *Astron. Astrophys.* 437, L39 (2005; arXiv:astro-ph/0406005); [arxiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/astro-ph/0406005) "Ultrarelativistic motion: inertial and tidal effects in Fermi coordinates," *Class. Quantum Grav.* 22, 195 (2005); "Explicit Fermi coordinates and tidal dynamics in de Sitter and Gödel spacetimes," *Phys. Rev. D* 74, 064019 (2006); and "Tidal Dynamics in Kerr Spacetime," arXiv:gr-qc/0602071 (2006). The result is robust *within the generalized-Jacobi / Fermi-coordinate framework* but is specific to that framework; it is not a universal feature of GR independent of foliation / observer. The Schwarzschild horizon is at GM/(r c²) = ½ trivially because r_s = 2GM/c². Critical damping in the *standard* sense of a damped harmonic oscillator is ζ = 1, not ζ = 1/√2; ζ = 1/√2 is the *Butterworth / maximally-flat* damping, which has special status in filter and control theory (see Wikipedia "Butterworth filter"; arXiv:2109.06034 explicitly discusses ζ = 1/√2 as the "optimal control value").

**Assessment of TEP's claim.** Each individual coincidence is real:
- Schwarzschild: r_s = 2GM/c² ⇒ GM/(r_s c²) = ½. Trivial.
- Damped oscillator: ζ = 1/√2 = Butterworth (maximally flat), **not** critical damping. TEP appears to *misidentify* ζ = 1/√2 with "critical damping"; classical critical damping is ζ = 1.
- Chicone–Mashhoon: V_c = c/√2 in the generalized Jacobi equation, robust within that approximation framework but not a universal "self-dual" principle.

There is **no published unifying principle** in the literature that "self-dual points of hyperbolic budget constraints coincide with physical transitions." The fact that f(y) = √(1−y²) has fixed point y = 1/√2 is a property of that particular map; that distinct physical conditions of the form A² + B² = 1 yield A = B = 1/√2 when A = B is **algebraic tautology**, not a unifying physical principle.

**Verdict:** c/√2 is a real GR result in a specific framework; the unification claim is unsupported coincidence-mongering, and the "critical damping" identification is incorrect (it should be "Butterworth" / "optimal damping").

### 5. SU(1,1) and squeezed-state hyperbolic geometry

**Established literature.** SU(1,1) is the structure group of two-mode squeezed states: Perelomov, "Coherent states for arbitrary Lie group," *Commun. Math. Phys.* 26, 222 (1972); Caves–Schumaker, "New formalism for two-photon quantum optics. I. Quadrature phases and squeezed states," *Phys. Rev. A* 31, 3068 (1985); [American Physical Society](https://link.aps.org/doi/10.1103/PhysRevA.31.3068) Schumaker–Caves, "New formalism for two-photon quantum optics. II. Mathematical foundation and compact notation," *Phys. Rev. A* 31, 3093 (1985); [APS Journals](https://journals.aps.org/pra/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevA.31.3093) ladder networks / Bogoliubov transformations are SU(1,1) elements (Shaterzadeh-Yazdi–Turner–Sanders, arXiv:0710.3205). Chi–Fraas–Tan (arXiv:2404.07905, 2024) give a modern statement: single-mode squeezed states are in bijection with points of the Poincaré disk, and quadratic-Hamiltonian evolution acts by Möbius transformations [arxiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.07905) — Theorem 1 of that paper: "the manifold of squeezed states is a model of hyperbolic geometry." [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/html/2404.07905v1) The Poincaré disk metric ds² = 4|dz|²/(1−|z|²)² has constant Gaussian curvature K = −1. The Bures metric on the qubit interior has K = +4 (Dittmann 1999 for higher-dim curvature; the qubit case is computable directly).

**Assessment of TEP's claim.** SU(1,1) as the symmetry group of passive lossless two-port scattering networks is **standard**. The Poincaré-disk picture for squeezed states is standard (Chi–Fraas–Tan 2024 give a clean modern treatment). Comparing K = −1 of the SU(1,1) disk against K = +4 of the Bures qubit metric "bi-metrically" is unusual but not wrong — it merely reflects that these two natural Riemannian structures on different (but related) manifolds (Gaussian states vs qubits) have opposite-sign constant curvature. No published priority claim.

**Verdict:** geometry standard, comparison unusual but not novel physics. Consistent.

### 6. MSS bound, OTOC, scrambling

**Established literature.** Maldacena–Shenker–Stanford, "A bound on chaos," *JHEP* 08, 106 (2016; arXiv:1503.01409), conjectured and argued λ_L ≤ 2π k_B T/ℏ for thermal OTOC growth, [arXiv +2](https://arxiv.org/abs/1503.01409) saturated by SYK and large-N holographic theories. [arxiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.10391) Follow-ups: Hashimoto et al. (arXiv:1610.06070) on horizon Lyapunov = surface gravity; [arXiv](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/1610.06070) Mezei–Stanford "butterfly cone" (arXiv:1908.03574) on velocity-dependent Lyapunov; [arxiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1908.03574) Halder "global symmetry" extension (arXiv:1908.05281); [arxiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1908.05281) holographic Kolmogorov–Sinai entropy growth dS/dt = Σ_{λ>0} 2π T (arXiv:2107.01300). [arxiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2107.01300) I found **no established result** stating d(C/B)/dt = −λ_L / ln 2 connecting channel capacity decay to the OTOC Lyapunov exponent. The factor 1/ln 2 is simply the conversion from natural log (nats) to log₂ (bits), so any nats-statement of an entropy/information decay rate λ_L acquires 1/ln 2 when written in bits/time.

**Assessment of TEP's claim.** Dimensionally trivial: if any entropy/information quantity decays at rate λ_L (in nats), expressing it in bits gives λ_L / ln 2. The MSS bound is a bound on λ_L, not a derivation of a capacity-decay rate. Holographic entropy-growth saturation conjectures (arXiv:2107.01300) sum over positive Lyapunov exponents; this is the closest established statement, and it concerns coarse-grained entropy near thermalization, not Shannon channel capacity.

**Verdict:** the equation is a unit conversion, not a new theorem; the connection to channel capacity is not established in the searched literature.

### 7. Riccati equations and quantum statistics

**Established literature.** The grand-canonical derivation of BE/FD distributions is textbook (e.g., Kok, *Advanced Quantum Mechanics* §8.4; Kolokoltsov arXiv:2004.03632, 2020). The Fermi–Dirac distribution is a logistic / sigmoid function; the corresponding ODE dn/ds = −n(1 − n) is the **logistic equation**, which is a special Riccati equation. Haldane, *Phys. Rev. Lett.* 67, 937 (1991), defined fractional exclusion statistics; [arxiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2511.02546) Wu, *Phys. Rev. Lett.* 73, 922 (1994), derived the distribution function from the implicit equation w(η)^g · [1 + w(η)]^{1−g} = exp[(ε − μ)/k_B T], with n = 1/(w + g) — the standard "Haldane–Wu equation." Kaniadakis–Quarati, *Phys. Rev. E* 49, 5103 (1994), and follow-ups (Rossani et al. arXiv:hep-th/9507119, 1995) derive interpolating distributions from a "kinetic exclusion-inclusion principle" [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/hep-th/9507119) — a nonlinear collision kernel of the form (1 ± a n)(1 ± b n′) — recovering BE (b = +1), FD (a = −1), and Haldane–Wu in special cases. Aringazin–Mazhitov (arXiv:cond-mat/0202274, 2002) derived Haldane–Wu combinatorially from integer-q occupation. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/cond-mat/0202274) Chaturvedi–Srinivasan (arXiv:hep-th/9608153) gave a Jack-polynomial interpolation between BE/FD/MB. [arxiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/hep-th/9608153)

**Assessment of TEP's claim.** That Riccati / logistic ODEs generate BE/FD as orbits is **already published** (Kaniadakis–Quarati 1994 and follow-ups). However, the specific claim that Haldane statistics arise *exactly* from a "convex (NSR-additive) composition of BE and FD channels" — e.g., 1/n(g) = g/n_FD + (1 − g)/n_BE or some other convex combination — does **not** in general reproduce the Wu equation w^g (1 + w)^{1−g} = e^{β(ε−μ)}. For g = 0, 1 (BE, FD endpoints) any reasonable interpolation matches by construction, but for intermediate g the convex-mean interpolation produces a *different* distribution from Wu's. The Kaniadakis–Quarati kinetic kernel matches Haldane–Wu only under specific kernel choices, not by general convex mixing.

**Verdict:** Riccati-orbit derivation is known (priority: Kaniadakis–Quarati 1994). The "NSR-additive" *exact* reproduction of Haldane is **probably incorrect for general g** — it agrees at endpoints but not in the interior unless one *defines* the composition to be the Wu equation, in which case the result is tautological.

### 8. Naming and prior programs

Searches for "Terminal Equivalence Principle," "Entrodynamics," and "Generalized Scale Relativity" in indexed databases (arXiv, Google Scholar, journal websites) yielded no peer-reviewed publications using those exact terms as a research program. **"Scale Relativity"** is Nottale's program (1984–present; *Fractal Space-Time and Microphysics*, World Scientific 1993; *Scale Relativity and Fractal Space-Time*, ICP 2011; arXiv:0812.3857) — a distinct, well-developed (though contested in mainstream physics) framework based on nondifferentiable fractal spacetime and scale-covariant derivatives. TEP should not be confused with Nottale's program; they share no specific technical machinery beyond the word "scale."

Budget equations x² + u = 1 with hyperbolic parametrization (x = sin θ, u = cos² θ, or x = tanh r) appear throughout physics: special-relativistic velocity composition (β² + 1/γ² = 1); Shannon's C = ½ log₂(1 + S/N), where S/(S + N) and N/(S + N) sum to 1; photon polarization Stokes parameters; reflection/transmission coefficients; etc. There is no published *unified organizing principle* of "budget equations" beyond the obvious normalization / probability-sum-to-one structure.

**Verdict:** TEP / Entrodynamics / Generalized Scale Relativity is not a recognized published program. Don't confuse with Nottale.

### 9. Kerr–Newman horizon identity

The textbook outer horizon of the Kerr–Newman metric (Wikipedia "Kerr–Newman metric"; Misner–Thorne–Wheeler §33; Wald *General Relativity* §12) is

  r_± = M ± √(M² − a² − Q²),

with sub-extremality requiring a² + Q² ≤ M². [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kerr%E2%80%93Newman_metric) [arxiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2411.00458) Rearranging:

  r_+ − M = √(M² − a² − Q²)
  ⇒ (r_+ − M)² = M² − a² − Q²
  ⇒ (r_+/M − 1)² + (a/M)² + (Q/M)² = 1.

This is an algebraic restatement of the horizon condition, exact for all sub-extremal Kerr–Newman black holes. Extremality (a² + Q² = M²) ↔ r_+/M = 1, [Scholarpedia](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/Kerr-Newman_metric) the "north pole" of this unit-sphere parametrization in (r_+/M − 1, j, q̂).

**Verdict:** trivial algebraic rewriting of a textbook formula; no novelty. Consistent.

## Recommendations

**Staged next steps for the assessment.**

1. **Stop treating restatements as discoveries.** Items 2 (Bures radial reduction), 9 (Kerr–Newman), 6 (1/ln 2 conversion), and the three-term passive-scattering budget in item 3 are textbook identities. Cite Dittmann 1999, Petz–Sudár 1996, Wald GR §12, and standard mesoscopic-physics references; do not present these as TEP-derived results. *Benchmark that would change this:* if TEP can derive any of them as a *consequence* of an independent first principle that also predicts a numerical value not present in the standard derivation, treat as novel; otherwise priority belongs to the standard literature.

2. **Pursue the moment-hierarchy QSL claim as the strongest candidate for genuine novelty.** Specifically, write up |dp_k/dt| ≤ 2k √(p_{2k−1} − p_k²) ΔH for k ≥ 3 as a direct corollary of Mandelstam–Tamm applied to ρ^{k−1}, citing Bolonek-Lasoń et al. arXiv:1902.02110 and Uzdin–Lutz–Kosloff arXiv:1408.1227 as immediate precursors and Hamazaki arXiv:2309.07301 as the closest 2024 paper (which bounds higher cumulants of observables, not Tr(ρ^k)). Rank-2 saturation should be cited as Anandan–Aharonov, *Phys. Rev. Lett.* 65, 1697 (1990) and Hörnedal et al. *Quantum* 7, 1055 (2023) — not claimed as new. *Benchmark that would change this:* submit to a QSL specialist (e.g., del Campo, Hörnedal, Pires, Hamazaki, Deffner); if they confirm the explicit Tr(ρ³) form is new, it is publishable as a corollary; if they point to a 2014–2024 paper that already states it, drop the novelty claim.

3. **Withdraw or substantially weaken the "self-dual point unification" claim (item 4).** ζ = 1/√2 is Butterworth / maximally-flat, *not* "critical damping" (ζ_critical = 1); fix this. The Schwarzschild GM/(rc²) = ½ is a tautology of r_s = 2GM/c². Chicone–Mashhoon's c/√2 is real but framework-specific. There is no published unifying principle; presenting these together as a TEP signature overclaims. *Benchmark:* find a peer-reviewed paper that derives at least two of these from a single principle. None exists in the searched literature.

4. **Withdraw or carefully restrict the Hodge/DtN claim (item 3).** The transmission/reflection/absorption budget is standard; the Hodge-decomposition identification is not. *Benchmark:* if TEP can produce an explicit projection from the port S-matrix data to the (closed, exact, harmonic) Hodge factors with a quantitative theorem (operator norms, isomorphism statement), this becomes a research claim; otherwise it is a heuristic and should be labeled as such.

5. **Verify the Haldane / NSR-additive claim numerically (item 7).** Compute n(g, β(ε − μ)) at, say, g = ½ and β(ε − μ) = 0.5, 1, 2, 5 from (i) the Wu equation w^g (1 + w)^{1−g} = e^{β(ε−μ)}, n = 1/(w + g), and (ii) the convex inverse-occupation combination 1/n = g/n_F + (1 − g)/n_B. If they disagree (very likely), the "exactly" claim must be retracted; Haldane statistics is recovered only at g = 0, 1. *Benchmark:* numerical agreement to ≤ 10⁻⁶ across the (g, β(ε−μ)) grid would support the claim; even one disagreement falsifies "exactly."

6. **Rename or carefully position the program (item 8).** "Terminal Equivalence Principle," "Entrodynamics," and "Generalized Scale Relativity" are not in the published literature — that is neutral, but the names "Scale Relativity" and "Equivalence Principle" risk confusion with Nottale's program and Einstein's principle. Either coin clearly distinct terminology, or position the program explicitly as a generalization / dialogue with these.

7. **Lead with the genuinely novel pieces; demote the coincidences.** A publishable version of this framework would consist of: (a) the moment-hierarchy QSL with explicit saturation conditions; (b) careful information-geometric statements that *cite* Petz, Dittmann, Slater rather than re-derive them; (c) explicit *predictions* — e.g., a measurable bound on dTr(ρ³)/dt in an open-system experiment (NMR à la Pires et al. 2023; cold-atom platforms) — that would distinguish TEP from existing QSLs. *Benchmark:* TEP earns predictive value only when it yields a falsifiable inequality not already implied by Pires–Cianciaruso 2016 + Uzdin–Lutz–Kosloff 2014 + Hamazaki 2024.

## Caveats

1. My searches were constrained to indexed databases (arXiv, journals, Google Scholar via web search) and ≈18 web searches plus one focused subagent. A more exhaustive search (Inspire-HEP, MathSciNet, control-theory and engineering literature) could surface earlier statements of the Tr(ρ³) bound or the convex-Haldane composition.

2. Without seeing TEP's full derivations I can only compare *stated* results, not full proofs. Several of the negative judgments above (item 3 Hodge/DtN, item 7 NSR-additive exactness) are conditional on the surface form of the claim; if TEP supplies precise statements, parts of those judgments could shift.

3. The literature on quantum speed limits is moving fast (2022–2025): papers I have not seen could pre-empt the moment-hierarchy claim. The closest current published work is Hamazaki arXiv:2309.07301 (*Commun. Phys.* 7, 361, 2024) on observable cumulants, which uses a different but related mechanism.

4. The "novelty in form" of the F = Tr(ρ³) − γ² Fisher-type identification is contingent on no prior paper having used this exact combination; my subagent's search did not find one, but absence of evidence is not evidence of absence.

5. The Butterworth-vs-critical-damping issue in item 4 may reflect terminology drift in the TEP framework rather than an error — but the standard meaning of "critical damping" is ζ = 1, and this should be clarified.

6. Chicone–Mashhoon's c/√2 is published in *Class. Quantum Grav.* (Letter) and replicated by the same authors in several follow-ups, including "Ultrarelativistic motion: inertial and tidal effects in Fermi coordinates," *Class. Quantum Grav.* 22, 195 (2005); "Explicit Fermi coordinates and tidal dynamics in de Sitter and Gödel spacetimes," *Phys. Rev. D* 74, 064019 (2006); and "Tidal Dynamics in Kerr Spacetime," arXiv:gr-qc/0602071 (2006). To my knowledge it has not been independently re-derived by other groups under different conventions; "robust" should mean "internally consistent within the generalized-Jacobi framework," not "independently confirmed across multiple frameworks."
